title: Scheduler

primary_when: request is about task scheduling, worker dispatch, or job queue design
request is about CPU parallel pipelines or deterministic task orchestration
must_not_own_when: request is mainly about Harmony patching, IMGUI, assembly dumps, or neural architecture definitions

solves: explicit task scheduling and worker orchestration
making parallel execution predictable and measurable
controlling contention, batching, and synchronization boundaries
smoothing workload over time so main-thread spikes do not dominate runtime behavior
enforcing explicit work budgets and shaping latency-versus-throughput tradeoffs
does_not_solve: parallelizing inherently tiny or cold work without evidence
hiding poor data layout behind more worker threads
feature ownership outside scheduling and execution flow

core_principles: Start from strict lifecycle order and explicit execution phases.
Keep task boundaries explicit and workload-shaped.
Separate scheduling logic from task logic.
Avoid data races through ownership and sequencing first, not through after-the-fact locking everywhere.
Prefer small frequent units of work over bursty oversized main-thread work when the system can be staged safely.
Assign explicit work budgets to runtime phases instead of allowing unlimited per-tick work.
Minimize synchronization scope and shared mutable state.
Choose throughput-oriented batching only when its latency profile is acceptable for the owning runtime phase.
Prefer deterministic dispatch where correctness, debugging, or profiling benefit from it.
Scale concurrency only after task granularity and data ownership are coherent.
constraints: Do not parallelize work units so small that scheduling overhead dominates.
Do not allow runtime methods on the main thread to accumulate large burst workloads by default.
Do not leave per-frame or per-tick work effectively unbounded when a budget can be enforced.
Do not hide shared mutable state behind convenience queue abstractions.
Avoid work-stealing or dynamic balancing complexity unless imbalance is a measured problem.
Any synchronization point must have a clear ownership and cost justification.
decision_rules: If a runtime method can either process a large batch immediately or spread the work across multiple smaller slices, prefer the sliced model unless latency or correctness requires the burst.
If the runtime phase has a hard responsiveness requirement, bias toward lower latency and bounded work even when peak throughput drops.
If throughput is the dominant goal and latency tolerance is higher, allow larger batches only if worst-case spike behavior remains inside the phase budget.
If a stage has clear independent partitions, prefer partitioned worker ownership over shared queues first.
If work units are irregular, compare queue contention against the imbalance cost before choosing dynamic scheduling.
If no explicit budget exists for a runtime phase, define one before optimizing the queueing strategy.
If main-thread work can stall frame progression, split it into bounded incremental units with explicit carry-over state.
If tasks communicate heavily, redesign data ownership before adding more threads.
If reproducibility matters for debugging or stateful systems, prefer deterministic scheduling and stable task order.
If a pipeline already has explicit stages, align scheduling boundaries with stage boundaries instead of ad hoc task spawning.

preferred_patterns: strict lifecycle phases with explicit ordering
explicit worker ownership and bounded queues
incremental processing with bounded per-tick work budgets
stage-based execution with clear handoff points
partitioned work sets with reduction or merge phases
stable instrumentation around queue depth, worker utilization, and contention points
avoid_patterns: burst-heavy runtime methods on the main thread
throughput-maximizing batches with no latency budget
task explosion from overly fine-grained units
shared global mutable state accessed from arbitrary workers
using more threads as a substitute for coherent workload design

recipes.name: Stage-Aligned Scheduler
recipes.when: A pipeline already has stable stages with clear input and output buffers.
steps: Define stage boundaries and handoff buffers.
Assign worker execution ownership per stage or partition.
Make synchronization explicit at stage boundaries only.
Measure queue depth, worker utilization, and latency under load.

recipes.name: Partition-First Worker Model
recipes.when: The workload can be split into stable independent partitions.
steps: Define the partition key and ownership model.
Bind each partition to a worker or bounded worker set.
Avoid cross-partition writes during the main processing pass.
Merge or reduce results in an explicit follow-up step if needed.

recipes.name: Bounded Incremental Main-Thread Work
recipes.when: A runtime method risks performing too much work at once and stalling the main thread.
steps: Define a strict execution phase where the work is allowed to run.
Split the total workload into bounded chunks with explicit carry-over state.
Process only the allowed budget per tick, frame, or update cycle.
Persist unfinished work for the next scheduled slice instead of forcing one burst pass.
Measure worst-case frame cost and adjust chunk size or staging model.

recipes.name: Latency-Biased Phase Budget
recipes.when: A runtime phase must stay responsive even if peak throughput is sacrificed.
steps: Define the maximum allowed work budget for the phase.
Set a bounded amount of work per tick, frame, or pass.
Carry overflow work forward through explicit queues or staged state.
Track worst-case latency and visible spikes rather than average throughput alone.
Increase throughput only if the latency target remains intact.

recipes.name: Throughput-Biased Batch Window
recipes.when: A stage benefits from batching and can tolerate higher completion latency within a bounded budget.
steps: Define the acceptable latency window for the batched work.
Accumulate work until the target batch size or time window is reached.
Process the batch in a predictable execution phase with explicit ownership.
Measure both throughput gain and worst-case delay introduced by batching.
Reduce batch size again if responsiveness regresses beyond the budget.

pitfalls: Allowing main-thread runtime methods to absorb unbounded work just because the logic is locally convenient.
Optimizing average throughput while ignoring phase budgets and worst-case latency.
Designing the scheduler before defining task granularity and data ownership.
Using dynamic queues to mask irregular or incoherent workload structure.
Allowing tasks to reach into shared global state freely.
Measuring thread count instead of actual throughput, latency, and contention.
review_checklist: Is lifecycle order explicit and stable?
Are task units large enough to justify scheduling overhead?
Are main-thread work units bounded to avoid visible spikes?
Does each important runtime phase have an explicit work budget?
Is the latency versus throughput tradeoff chosen deliberately rather than accidentally?
Are worker ownership and synchronization boundaries explicit?
Does the scheduler avoid unnecessary shared mutable state?
Is the dispatch model aligned with the dataflow or stage model?
Are contention and queue behavior measurable in the design?

references.title: Scheduler
references.interpretation: Use for task granularity, contention analysis, and scheduler review.

references.title: Performance Domain Foundations
references.interpretation: Use for optimization discipline and cost model framing.
